text: Hello, I'm sharing a RTAP left inguinal hernia repair. This is a very symptomatic inguinal hernia in a young man.
The hernia is visible in the distance on the left-hand side of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to develop the preperitoneal plane, revealing the thin peritoneum, and proceeds pre-transverse salus due to the delicate nature of the tissue.
The hernia is visible on the back of the rectus muscle.
The surgeon begins medial dissection, progressing towards the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, using careful technique to navigate the anatomical structures.
The hernia is visible on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the preperitoneal plane laterally and the pre-transverse salus or retro-rectus plane medially. The transverse salus fascia is visible attached to the semilunar line.
The surgeon dissects below the arcuate line to re-enter the preperitoneal space, completing the transition from the pre-transverse salus plane.
The surgeon sweeps the chord structures laterally, revealing their position relative to the rectus muscle.
The surgeon decides to abandon the hernia sac that extends towards the scrotum, avoiding extensive dissection within the inguinal canal.
The surgeon approaches the end of the dissection, avoiding further entry into the inguinal canal.

The surgeon ensures complete clearance of the cord structures and proceeds to amputate the hernia sac at the specified level. The surgeon continues to parietalize the tissue to create space for the placement of the inferior portion of the mesh.
The obturator space is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the surgical field to create additional space for the mesh, ensuring it extends slightly across the midline.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field to identify the presence of a cord lipoma, examining the area around the spermatic cord.
The surgeon presents the critical view, showcasing the anatomical structures relevant to the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon places the mesh into the prepared pocket and begins to unfold it in a serial manner, ensuring it covers the necessary area.
The surgeon proceeds to close the surgical flap, completing the coverage over the previously placed mesh.
The surgeon prepares to close the hole created by the amputation of the hernia sac.
These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes skin to skin. There was nothing particularly challenging about this case. It certainly can be done faster with a laparoscopic TEP.

I think in a young healthy individual the benefits of a speedy surgery are negligible and surgeon comfort and preference has validity. I'm a fan of the TAP approach, and I'm a fan of the robotic platform, just due to the dexterity and comfortability of the procedure.
That being said, I'm very comfortable with all forms of laparoscopic, TEP, TAP, ETEP, and robotic, and will adjust based upon what the case calls for.
The surgeon closes the defect in the hernia sac, ensuring the tissue is securely sutured to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon suspends the hernia sac to the peritoneal closure, securing the closure of the defect created by the amputation of the sac.
And that concludes the procedure. I hope you enjoyed.
